notes.anchor: from the first shore of Greece all the way to Egypt
notes.note: Cicero sketches the arc of the eastern Mediterranean now (he hopes) held by republican commanders: Brutus in Greece and Macedonia, Cassius in Syria, and their allies down to the borders of Egypt.

notes.anchor: the entire decisive issue of the whole war seemed to rest on D. Brutus
notes.note: Decimus Brutus (a different Brutus from Marcus) was besieged by Antony in Mutina; relieving him was the central objective of the consuls' campaign in early 43 BC.
Mutina (modern Modena) in Cisalpine Gaul, where Decimus Brutus was blockaded by Antony; the siege gave its name to the Mutina War of 43 BC.

notes.anchor: Our Hirtius was at Claterna, Caesar at Forum Cornelii
notes.note: The consul Hirtius and the young Caesar (Octavian, leading his private army) were posted at towns on the Via Aemilia closing in on Antony; here "Caesar" is Octavian, not the dead dictator.

notes.anchor: Pansa had levied great forces from the muster of Italy
notes.note: The consul C. Vibius Pansa was raising fresh troops at Rome through the dilectus (the levy or muster) across Italy, to reinforce the campaign against Antony.
The inhabitants of Transpadane Gaul (Cisalpine Gaul north of the Po) stood in a hereditary client relationship to Cassius's family and so favored the republican cause.

notes.anchor: With Servius Sulpicius's death we have lost a great bulwark
notes.note: Servius Sulpicius Rufus, the eminent jurist and consular, died while serving on the senatorial embassy to Antony at Mutina; his loss removed a steadying voice from the Senate.

notes.anchor: from those regions of the East the light of your courage shine forth
notes.note: A closing prayer that Cassius, campaigning in Syria and the eastern provinces, will reveal his successes; "those regions" (istis Orientis partibus) gestures eastward to where Cassius then was.
